blocks.content: The studio gathered dust. The pencils sat exactly where he had left them the morning she died. The commissioned landscape, still unfinished, still leaning against the wall.
He went to the studio sometimes. He stood in it. He looked at his tools.
He couldn't make the connection. The part of him that drew was the same part that had held her — the part that noticed everything and kept the account — and that part was occupied completely.
Time moved the way it moves when you are carrying something enormous. Not slowly, not quickly — strangely. Days that were the length of years. Weeks that vanished.
He kept waking up and reaching for her.
He kept turning around in rooms to say something to her, arriving at the silence of the house, and standing there for a moment in the space where she should have been.
He was learning that grief was not a single thing. It was a thousand small discoveries throughout the day that she was gone. Each one its own.
